claim: The Transformer proposed a sequence transduction architecture based on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolution in the core model.
blockers: Do not claim the Transformer was the first attention model or that it alone caused later LLM products.

claim: Scaled dot-product attention uses query, key, and value projections to compute attention weights and weighted value mixtures.
blockers: Do not imply the model has human intention or consciousness.

claim: The Transformer uses self-attention inside encoder/decoder stacks, with masked self-attention in the decoder for autoregressive sequence generation.
blockers: Do not treat this as a full modern decoder-only LLM implementation description.

claim: Multi-head attention lets the model learn multiple attention subspaces/patterns in parallel within a layer.
blockers: Do not assign stable human-interpretable functions to heads without a separate interpretability source.

claim: The Google Research blog framed the Transformer as using self-attention to model dependencies without regard to their distance in input or output sequences.
prose_permission: Allowed as public framing with source cue.
blockers: Do not convert this into a claim that distance never matters computationally or behaviorally.

claim: Because attention alone lacks order, the Transformer used positional encodings to inject sequence-order information.
location: manuscript/02-attention-catches-fire.md:Position: The Thing Attention Does Not Know
blockers: Do not imply all later LLMs use the exact same positional scheme.

claim: Transformer blocks combine attention and feed-forward transformations with residual/layer-normalization structure and can be stacked as a reusable architecture grammar.
blockers: Do not claim every modern model preserves the original block unchanged.

claim: The Transformer removal of recurrence made more parallel computation over sequence positions possible during training, helping the architecture fit accelerator-era scaling.
blockers: Do not claim parallelism alone explains dominance or later product success.

claim: GPT-1, GPT-2, and GPT-3 belong to the later generative Transformer lineage and should be treated as downstream chapter facts, not as claims licensed only by the Transformer paper.
blockers: Do not expand into GPT benchmark, parameter, or release claims in Chapter 2 without row-specific extraction.

claim: The chapter queues three diagrams: annotated Transformer block, recurrence-versus-self-attention comparison, and optional model-stack view.
blockers: No visual is counted as created until an asset manifest row and actual SVG/diagram exist.

claim: The chapter's humility section blocks claims that the Transformer solved truth, grounding, memory, hallucination, prompt injection, long-context cost, or faithful explainability through attention weights.
blockers: Do not remove during polish unless equivalent limitations stay visible.

claim: The chapter explains the bridge from the original encoder-decoder Transformer to decoder-style autoregressive GPT language modeling without treating the original paper as a chatbot paper.
blockers: Keep detailed GPT chronology, parameters, benchmarks, and product claims in Chapter 5 unless separately audited.

claim: The chapter defines the Transformer as an LLM substrate because it combined relational context, modular blocks, accelerator-friendly parallelism, and natural fit with pretraining recipes.
support_quality: synthesis from primary paper, official commentary, and GPT-1 source
blockers: Do not claim eternal dominance or that hardware alone selected the architecture.
